content.text: A higher-order function (HOF) is a function that either accepts other functions as arguments, returns a function, or both. This concept is everywhere in React:
are all higher-order functions. The React hooks
, event handler patterns, and the HOC (Higher-Order Component) pattern all rely on the same idea.
— you can store them in variables, pass them as arguments, return them from functions, and put them in arrays or objects. This is what makes higher-order functions possible:
are the most familiar higher-order functions. You can write your own:
A HOF can return a new function — often called a
pattern. The returned function closes over the outer function's parameters:
Because functions are values, you can combine multiple HOFs to build pipelines where data flows through a series of transformations:

const users = [
  { name: 'Alice', age: 28, active: true },
  { name: 'Bob',   age: 22, active: false },
  { name: 'Carol', age: 35, active: true },
  { name: 'Dave',  age: 19, active: true },
];

// Pipeline: filter → map → sort
const result = users
  .filter(u => u.active)           // keep active users
  .filter(u => u.age >= 21)        // keep adults
  .map(u => u.name.toUpperCase())  // extract and format names
  .sort();                          // alphabetical

console.log(result); // ['ALICE', 'CAROL']
When you need to pass extra data to an event handler, a common pattern is to create a handler using a HOF:
A Higher-Order Component is a function that takes a component and returns an enhanced component — the React application of the HOF pattern:
HOCs were the dominant pattern before hooks. Today, custom hooks accomplish the same goal more cleanly, but HOCs are still found in libraries like
Custom hooks are functions that return values and functions — the same idea as a factory HOF, applied to React state and effects:
Calling the function instead of passing it
Re-creating factory results on every render
A higher-order function accepts a function as an argument, returns a function, or both. JavaScript's built-in array methods (
Functions are first-class values in JavaScript: they can be assigned to variables, passed as arguments, returned from functions, and stored in data structures.
In React, HOFs appear as Higher-Order Components (HOCs), event handler factories, and
. Custom hooks are conceptually the same pattern applied to stateful logic.
Chaining HOFs (filter → map → sort) creates readable, declarative data transformation pipelines.
